expanded_description: A clear introduction to xylem tissue for secondary school biology. This covers how xylem transports water and minerals from roots to leaves, the types of cells involved (tracheids and vessel elements), the physical forces that pull water upward (cohesion-tension theory), and how xylem structure helps plants survive drought.

core_concept.principle: Xylem is the plant's water transport system. It is made of dead, hollow cells that form tubes from roots to leaves. Water is pulled upward by evaporation from leaves, not pushed from roots.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine trying to get water to the top of a 100-meter tree. There is no pump at the bottom. The tree does not have a heart. So how does water get up there? The answer is xylem—a specialized tissue that acts like a network of drinking straws running from the roots to the leaves.

Xylem is made of dead cells. This is important because the cells need to be empty—hollow tubes—so water can flow through them easily. The walls of these cells are thickened with a substance called lignin, which makes them strong and waterproof. Lignin is what makes wood hard.

There are two main types of water-conducting cells in xylem. Tracheids are long, tapered cells with small openings (pits) in their walls. Water moves from one tracheid to the next through these pits. Vessel elements are shorter and wider. They line up end to end, and the walls between them break down, forming long, continuous pipes called vessels. Vessels are found in flowering plants (angiosperms); tracheids are found in all vascular plants, including conifers and ferns.

How does water get from the roots to the top of a tree? The key is evaporation from the leaves, called transpiration. When water evaporates from the leaf surface, it creates a suction. This suction pulls on the water in the xylem. Water molecules are sticky—they stick to each other (cohesion) and to the walls of the xylem (adhesion). So when one water molecule is pulled up, it pulls the one behind it, and so on, all the way down to the roots. This is called the cohesion-tension theory.

Xylem also carries dissolved minerals from the soil. These minerals are taken up by the roots and carried upward with the water. Without xylem, plants could not get water from the soil to their leaves, and they could not get the nutrients they need to grow.

Xylem is not just for transport. It also provides support. The lignin in xylem cell walls makes the plant rigid. That is why trees can stand tall—their xylem (wood) holds them up.

Understanding xylem helps us understand how plants survive drought, how trees grow so tall, and how we can improve crops for better water use.

image.alt: A diagram showing xylem as tubes running from roots to leaves, with water moving upward
image.caption: Xylem: Tubes of dead cells running from roots to leaves. Water is pulled upward by evaporation from leaves (transpiration). Lignin in cell walls provides strength and prevents collapse.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Challenge of Moving Water Uphill
sections.introduction:
Imagine you are a tree. You are rooted in one spot. Your leaves are high in the air, catching sunlight. But you need water to photosynthesize, and the water is down in the soil. You cannot walk to a stream. You cannot pump water with a heart. How do you get water from your roots to your leaves?

This is one of the great challenges of being a plant. For animals, moving fluids around the body is easy—we have a heart that pumps blood. Plants have no heart. Yet a redwood tree can move hundreds of liters of water a day from its roots to its leaves, 100 meters above the ground.

The solution is xylem. Xylem is a tissue made of hollow, dead cells that form continuous tubes from the roots to the leaves. These tubes are like drinking straws. When water evaporates from the leaves, it creates suction that pulls water up through the xylem. The plant does not push water; the leaves pull it.

This system is passive—it uses no energy. The energy comes from the sun, which heats the leaves and causes water to evaporate. This is why plants need sunlight not just for photosynthesis, but also for water transport.

Xylem is also structural. The same cells that carry water are thickened with lignin, a hard, waterproof material. Lignin is what makes wood hard. A tree's trunk is mostly old xylem (wood), which supports the tree against gravity.

Xylem evolved about 420 million years ago, in the first vascular plants. Before xylem, plants were small and lived in wet places. With xylem, plants could grow tall and live in drier environments. The evolution of xylem was a key step in the colonization of land by plants.

Understanding xylem helps us understand how plants work, how forests grow, and how crops respond to drought. It also helps us appreciate the cleverness of evolution: a transport system with no moving parts, powered by the sun.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing a tall tree with water moving from roots to leaves through xylem, and a close-up of xylem cells
introduction_image.caption: Xylem allows plants to grow tall. Water is pulled from roots to leaves by evaporation, not pushed by a pump. Lignin in xylem walls provides strength.

key_concept.definition: Xylem is a tissue of dead, hollow cells that transport water and minerals from roots to leaves. Water movement is driven by transpiration (evaporation from leaves) and explained by the cohesion-tension theory.
key_concept.context:
Xylem has two types of water-conducting cells. Tracheids are long, tapered cells found in all vascular plants. Water moves between tracheids through small openings called pits. Vessel elements are shorter, wider cells found only in flowering plants (angiosperms). They line up end to end, and the walls between them break down to form continuous pipes called vessels.

Water moves through xylem because of transpiration. When water evaporates from the leaves, it creates a suction that pulls water up from the roots. Water molecules are cohesive (they stick to each other) and adhesive (they stick to the walls of the xylem). This keeps the water column intact, even under tension.

Xylem also provides support. The cell walls are thickened with lignin, a hard, waterproof material. Lignin prevents the xylem tubes from collapsing under the tension of water being pulled upward. It also makes wood strong enough to hold up a tree.

Xylem is passive—it uses no energy for transport. The energy comes from the sun, which heats the leaves and drives evaporation. This is why plants need sunlight even when they are not photosynthesizing; the sun also powers their water transport system.

image.alt: Diagrams of tracheids (long, tapered with pits) and vessel elements (shorter, wider with perforation plates)
image.caption: Xylem cells: Tracheids (pits allow water between cells) and Vessel elements (form continuous pipes). Both are dead, hollow, and have lignified walls.

sections.key_concept: Xylem has two types of water-conducting cells. Tracheids are safe but slow; vessel elements are fast but vulnerable to air bubbles. This trade-off affects how different plants survive drought.

classifications.type: Tracheids: The Safe and Steady Cells
classifications.value: Understanding tracheids explains how conifers and ferns transport water and survive harsh conditions.
characteristics: Long, tapered cells (1-5 mm long, very narrow)
Lignified walls with pits (small openings) for water to move between cells
Found in all vascular plants; the only conducting cells in conifers and ferns
Water moves slowly but safely—air bubbles are contained
classifications.behavior:
Tracheids are the original water-conducting cells. They are long and thin, with tapered ends that overlap. Water moves from one tracheid to the next through pits—small, thin areas in the cell wall where only the primary wall remains.

In conifers (pine, spruce, fir), the pits have a special structure called a torus-margo. The torus is a thick disk in the center of the pit. The margo is a porous, web-like ring around it. When a water column breaks (forming an air bubble), the pressure difference sucks the torus against the pit opening, sealing it. This prevents the air bubble from spreading to neighboring cells.

This makes tracheids safe. They can survive drought and freezing better than vessels. But they are also slow. Water has to travel through many pits to move from one tracheid to the next, which slows it down. This is why conifers grow more slowly than many flowering trees.

Tracheids also provide support. Their overlapping arrangement and thick, lignified walls make them strong. In conifers, the wood is almost entirely tracheids—there are no vessel elements.

examples.process: Torus-Margo Pits: How Conifers Survive Drought
examples.explanation:
If you look at pine wood under a microscope, you will see rows of tracheids with pits in their walls. Each pit is like a tiny valve.

When the tracheid is full of water, the torus sits in the center of the pit, and water flows around it through the porous margo. The water moves easily from one tracheid to the next.

But if an air bubble forms in one tracheid (from drought or freezing), the pressure difference sucks the torus against the pit opening. The torus seals the pit, and the air bubble cannot spread to the neighboring tracheid.

This is why conifers can survive freezing winters and dry summers. The torus-margo pit prevents cavitation (air bubbles) from spreading. A conifer may lose some tracheids to air bubbles, but the rest keep working. This safety comes at a cost: the pits also slow water flow, which is why conifers grow slowly.

image.alt: A diagram of a conifer pit showing the torus (central disk) and margo (porous ring) in open and sealed positions
image.caption: Torus-margo pits: In conifers, the torus (disk) floats in the center when water flows; if an air bubble forms, pressure seals the torus against the pit opening, preventing spread of the bubble.

classifications.type: Vessel Elements: The Fast but Fragile Cells
classifications.value: Understanding vessel elements explains how flowering plants transport water quickly and why they are more vulnerable to drought.
characteristics: Short, wide cells (0.1-1 mm long, 20-500 µm wide)
End walls break down to form perforation plates (openings)
Line up end to end to form continuous vessels (long tubes)
Found only in flowering plants (angiosperms) and a few other groups
classifications.behavior:
Vessel elements are the speedsters of the plant world. They are wider than tracheids and stacked end to end. The walls between them break down, forming perforation plates—openings that allow water to flow straight through without having to pass through pits.

This makes vessels much faster than tracheids. According to the Hagen-Poiseuille law (which describes flow through pipes), flow rate increases with the fourth power of the radius. A vessel twice as wide as a tracheid can carry 16 times as much water. This is why flowering plants can grow faster and have higher photosynthetic rates than conifers.

But there is a trade-off. Wide vessels are more vulnerable to cavitation—air bubbles forming under tension. Once an air bubble forms in a vessel, it can block the entire tube. And because vessels are long, a single bubble can disable a lot of xylem.

Flowering plants have evolved several ways to deal with this. Some have many parallel vessels, so if one is blocked, others can take over. Some have narrow vessels that are safer (but slower). Some have a mix of wide vessels for spring (when water is plentiful) and narrow vessels for summer (when drought is likely).

examples.process: Perforation Plates: The End Walls of Vessels
examples.explanation:
Look at a vessel element under a microscope, and you will see that the ends are different from the sides. The side walls are thick and lignified, with pits. The end walls are thin and have large openings—perforations.

There are different types of perforation plates. Simple perforation plates are just one big hole. This allows water to flow fastest. Multiple perforation plates have several openings separated by bars (scalariform) or a net (reticulate). These are slower but provide more structural support.

In most flowering plants, the perforation plates are simple. This is an adaptation for fast water flow. In some plants (like maples), the perforation plates are scalariform—a bit slower but stronger. In very fast-growing trees (like willows), the vessels are wide and have simple perforations, maximizing flow.

image.alt: Diagrams of simple perforation (one large hole) and scalariform perforation (multiple bars) in vessel elements
image.caption: Perforation plates: Simple (one big opening, fast flow) and Scalariform (multiple bars, slower but stronger). Flowering plants use both, depending on need for speed vs. strength.

examples.explanation:
If you look at the end of an oak log, you will see rings. Each ring is one year's growth. In oak (a ring-porous tree), the earlywood (spring growth) has very wide vessels. The latewood (summer growth) has narrow vessels or fibers.

Why? In spring, water is plentiful. The tree can afford to make wide vessels for fast flow. It needs to push new leaves out quickly to capture sunlight. In summer, water is scarcer. The tree makes narrow vessels that are safer—they are less likely to cavitate in dry conditions.

In maple (a diffuse-porous tree), the vessels are all about the same size throughout the ring. Maple grows more slowly in spring but keeps growing steadily through summer. It does not have the same burst of spring growth as oak.

This difference affects how the trees respond to drought. Oak may lose most of its water transport capacity if the earlywood vessels cavitate. Maple loses less. This is why maples are often found in drier areas than oaks, even though oaks grow faster in good conditions.

image.alt: Cross-sections of oak (ring-porous, large earlywood vessels) and maple (diffuse-porous, uniform vessels)
image.caption: Ring-porous wood (oak): Large vessels in spring (fast flow), narrow in summer (safe). Diffuse-porous wood (maple): Uniform vessels all year. Different strategies for different climates.

principles.statement: Water is pulled up through xylem by tension created when water evaporates from leaves. Cohesion (water sticking to water) and adhesion (water sticking to xylem walls) keep the water column intact.
principles.application:
When a leaf loses water through its stomata (transpiration), the water in the leaf xylem is pulled outward. This creates tension—negative pressure—in the xylem. Because water molecules are cohesive (they stick to each other by hydrogen bonds), this tension is transmitted all the way down to the roots. The entire water column is pulled upward like a rope.

Adhesion helps, too. Water molecules stick to the hydrophilic walls of the xylem. This prevents the water column from slipping backward. It also helps the column resist breaking under tension.

The tension in xylem can be very high. In a well-watered tree, the tension is about -1 to -2 MPa (megapascals). This is like pulling on a straw with enough force to lift a column of water 100-200 meters. In a drought, the tension can reach -5 to -10 MPa or more. If the tension gets too high, the water column can break, forming an air bubble (cavitation). This is like a bubble forming in a straw when you suck too hard.

The cohesion-tension theory was proposed over 100 years ago and is supported by many lines of evidence. Xylem sap is under negative pressure (you can measure it with a pressure bomb). The sap does not come out of a cut stem—it is sucked in. Dye moves upward only when leaves are transpiring. And the theoretical maximum height of a tree is limited by the point at which tension would cause the water column to break (about 120-130 meters).
principles.example:
You can see cohesion in action with a simple experiment. Fill a glass with water and dip a paper towel into it. The water climbs up the paper towel. This is capillary action, driven by cohesion and adhesion. But in a tree, the force is much stronger because water is being pulled from above, not just drawn up by capillary forces.

A better analogy is a straw. When you suck on a straw, you create negative pressure in your mouth. This pulls the water up. A tree's leaves are doing the same thing, but instead of your mouth, it is the evaporation of water from leaf cells that creates the suction.

The tallest trees in the world are redwoods, about 115 meters tall. This is close to the theoretical limit for water transport by cohesion-tension. Any taller, and the tension would be so great that the water column would break (cavitate) even in well-watered conditions.
principles.analogy: Cohesion-tension is like a rope of water being pulled from the top. Each water molecule holds onto the next (cohesion), and the rope holds onto the walls of the pipe (adhesion). If you pull too hard, the rope can break (cavitation). But if the rope is strong, you can pull water up a very long way.

image.alt: A diagram showing water evaporating from leaves, creating tension that pulls water up through xylem
image.caption: Cohesion-tension: Water evaporates from leaves → tension (negative pressure) → water pulled up xylem. Cohesion (water sticks to water) and adhesion (water sticks to xylem walls) keep the column intact.

principles.principle: Water Potential Drives Flow from Soil to Air
principles.statement: Water moves from high water potential to low water potential. The gradient from soil (high) to atmosphere (very low) drives water through the plant.
principles.application:
Water potential is a measure of the free energy of water. It is measured in units of pressure (megapascals, MPa). Pure water at atmospheric pressure has a water potential of 0 MPa. Adding solutes lowers water potential (makes it more negative). Applying pressure raises water potential (makes it more positive).

In the soil-plant-atmosphere continuum, water potentials are: Soil: -0.1 to -1.0 MPa (depending on how wet it is). Root xylem: -0.5 to -1.0 MPa. Stem xylem: -1.0 to -2.0 MPa. Leaf xylem: -1.5 to -3.0 MPa. Atmosphere: -100 MPa or less (dry air).

Water moves down this gradient, from less negative to more negative. The steepest part of the gradient is between the leaf and the atmosphere. That is where transpiration creates the most tension.

When soil dries, the water potential at the roots becomes more negative. The gradient from soil to roots gets smaller, so water moves more slowly. The plant responds by closing its stomata, which reduces transpiration and raises leaf water potential. This restores the gradient but also reduces photosynthesis.

If the soil gets too dry, the water potential at the roots may become more negative than at the leaves. Then water would flow out of the plant into the soil—the plant would wilt and eventually die.
principles.example: Imagine a bucket of water with a hole in the bottom. The water level (potential) is higher in the bucket than outside, so water flows out. As the bucket empties, the water level drops, and flow slows. When the bucket is empty, flow stops. The plant is like a bucket of water connected to a pipe. The soil is the bucket; the atmosphere is the outside. The plant can close its stomata (like putting a finger over the hole) to slow the flow when the bucket gets low.
principles.analogy: Water potential is like elevation. Water flows downhill, from high elevation (high potential) to low elevation (low potential). Soil is the top of the hill; atmosphere is the bottom. The plant is the river. When the hill is tall (wet soil), water flows fast. When the hill is short (dry soil), water flows slow.

image.alt: A diagram showing water potential values from soil (-0.1 MPa) to leaf (-1.5 MPa) to atmosphere (-100 MPa)
image.caption: Water potential gradient: Soil (-0.1) → Root (-0.5) → Stem (-1.0) → Leaf (-1.5) → Air (-100). Water flows down the gradient, from high (less negative) to low (more negative).

principles.principle: Efficiency vs. Safety: The Xylem Trade-off
principles.statement: Wide xylem vessels transport water fast but are vulnerable to air bubbles (cavitation). Narrow vessels are safe but slow. Plants balance these trade-offs based on their environment.
principles.application:
There is a fundamental trade-off in xylem design. Wide vessels have high hydraulic conductivity—they can move a lot of water quickly. This is good for fast growth and high photosynthesis. But wide vessels are also more vulnerable to cavitation. When tension gets high (during drought), the water column can break, forming an air bubble. Once formed, the bubble blocks the vessel.

Narrow vessels are the opposite. They are slow, but they are less likely to cavitate. And if a narrow vessel does cavitate, the loss of conductivity is smaller because the vessel is small.

Plants have evolved different strategies. Ring-porous trees (like oak) have wide vessels in spring (efficient) and narrow vessels in summer (safe). Diffuse-porous trees (like maple) have uniform vessels (moderate efficiency and safety). Conifers have only tracheids (safe but slow). Desert plants have very narrow vessels (very safe, but slow).

This trade-off affects where plants can live. Fast-growing trees with wide vessels do well in wet environments. Slow-growing trees with narrow vessels can survive in dry environments. Understanding this trade-off helps us predict how forests will respond to climate change.
principles.example: Compare an oak and a pine. Oak (ring-porous) has wide vessels in its earlywood. In a wet spring, it can transport water very fast and grow quickly. But if a drought hits in summer, those wide vessels may cavitate, and the tree may suffer. Pine (tracheids only) has narrow, safe cells. It grows slowly, but it can survive drought better. This is why pines are often found in dry, sandy soils where oaks cannot grow.
principles.analogy: Xylem efficiency vs. safety is like highway design. Wide highways (wide vessels) let traffic flow fast, but one accident (cavitation) can cause a huge jam. Narrow highways (narrow vessels) are slow, but accidents have less impact. Cities (wet environments) build wide highways. Rural areas (dry environments) build narrow roads. Plants do the same thing.

image.alt: A graph showing that wide vessels have high conductivity but low cavitation resistance; narrow vessels have low conductivity but high resistance
image.caption: Efficiency vs. safety trade-off: Wide vessels (fast flow, vulnerable to air bubbles). Narrow vessels (slow flow, resistant to air bubbles). Plants balance based on their environment.

components.definition: Xylem is made of tracheary elements (tracheids and vessel elements) for transport, fibers for support, and parenchyma for storage and repair.
components.interpretation: Flow rate depends strongly on vessel radius. Doubling the radius increases flow 16 times. This is why wide vessels are so efficient. But wide vessels are also more vulnerable to cavitation. This is the fundamental trade-off in xylem design.

image.alt: A diagram of xylem tissue showing vessel elements, tracheids, fibers, and parenchyma
image.caption: Xylem components: Vessel elements and tracheids (transport), Fibers (support), Parenchyma (storage, repair). All work together to move water and support the plant.

categories.type: Xylem Sap: What Flows in the Tubes
categories.description: Xylem sap is mostly water with dissolved minerals, hormones, and a small amount of organic compounds. It is dilute and flows passively.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Xylem sap is about 95-99% water. The rest is dissolved minerals (potassium, calcium, magnesium, nitrate, phosphate), hormones (ABA, cytokinins), and a small amount of organic compounds (amino acids, sugars). The sap is dilute—about 0.1 to 0.5% solutes by weight.

Minerals enter the xylem in the roots. Root cells actively pump ions into the xylem, lowering the water potential and causing water to follow by osmosis. This creates root pressure, which can push water up a short distance (especially at night when transpiration is low).

Calcium is an important mineral in xylem sap. It is not mobile in phloem, so it must be delivered to growing tissues by xylem. If transpiration is low (high humidity), calcium may not reach developing fruits, causing problems like blossom end rot in tomatoes.

Hormones in xylem sap carry signals from roots to shoots. For example, when roots sense drought, they produce ABA (abscisic acid), which travels in the xylem to the leaves and causes stomata to close. This is how the plant coordinates its response to dry soil.

examples.explanation:
Calcium is an essential plant nutrient. It is used in cell walls and signaling. But calcium is not mobile in phloem—once it is deposited, it cannot be moved to another part of the plant. So the only way calcium gets to new growth is through xylem.

If transpiration is low (cloudy weather, high humidity), the xylem flow slows, and less calcium reaches developing fruits. The result is blossom end rot—a dark, sunken spot on the bottom of tomatoes, peppers, or melons. The fruit has grown faster than calcium could be delivered.

The solution is to ensure consistent water availability. Mulching, watering regularly, and avoiding high-nitrogen fertilizers (which promote rapid growth) can help prevent calcium deficiency.

image.alt: Tomato with blossom end rot, and a diagram showing calcium transport in xylem
image.caption: Calcium transport: Calcium moves only in xylem, not phloem. Low transpiration (high humidity) reduces calcium delivery to fruits, causing blossom end rot.

categories.description: Lignin is a hard, waterproof polymer that strengthens xylem walls, allowing them to resist collapse under tension and support the plant.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Lignin is a complex polymer made from alcohol monomers (monolignols). It is deposited in the secondary cell walls of xylem cells after the cell has stopped growing. Lignin fills the spaces between cellulose microfibrils, making the wall rigid and waterproof.

Without lignin, xylem cells would collapse under the tension of water being pulled upward. They would also be vulnerable to fungal attack. Lignin is what makes wood hard and durable.

Lignin is also what makes paper from wood. In papermaking, lignin is removed because it yellows over time. High-lignin wood is used for construction; low-lignin wood is used for paper.

The pattern of lignin deposition varies. In primary xylem (which forms while the cell is still elongating), lignin is deposited in rings or spirals so the cell can stretch. In secondary xylem (wood), lignin is deposited in a continuous layer, maximizing strength.

examples.explanation:
Xylem cells have different wall thickening patterns depending on when they are formed. In cells that are still growing, the secondary wall is deposited in rings or spirals. This allows the cell to elongate after the wall is deposited. You can see these patterns in the xylem of young stems.

In cells that have stopped growing, the secondary wall is continuous. This is what you see in wood—thick, lignified walls with only small pits for water to pass through.

The pattern affects strength. Ringed and spiral walls are weaker but more flexible. Continuous walls are stronger but rigid. This is why young stems are flexible and mature wood is stiff.

image.alt: Diagrams of xylem wall thickening patterns: annular (rings), spiral, scalariform (ladder), pitted
image.caption: Wall thickening patterns: Annular/Spiral (flexible, allows growth) → Scalariform/Pitted (strong, rigid). Patterns match the cell's function and stage of development.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Understanding Xylem Tension and Flow

worked_examples.title: Calculating Water Potential and Flow Rate
worked_examples.problem: A plant has a soil water potential of -0.2 MPa and a leaf water potential of -1.2 MPa. The hydraulic conductivity of the plant (a measure of how easily water flows) is 4 × 10⁻⁸ kg·s⁻¹·MPa⁻¹. (a) Calculate the water flow rate from soil to leaf. (b) If drought reduces soil water potential to -0.8 MPa, what happens to flow? (c) If the plant closes its stomata, raising leaf water potential to -0.6 MPa, what happens to flow?

step_by_step.calculation: Water flow rate (J) = Hydraulic conductivity (K) × Water potential gradient (ΔΨ). ΔΨ = Ψₛₒᵢₗ - Ψₗₑₐf. A positive ΔΨ means water flows from soil to leaf. A negative ΔΨ means water flows out of the plant (wilting).
ΔΨ = (-0.2) - (-1.2) = 1.0 MPa. J = 4 × 10⁻⁸ × 1.0 = 4 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s. Water is flowing from soil to leaf.
New ΔΨ = (-0.8) - (-1.2) = 0.4 MPa. J = 4 × 10⁻⁸ × 0.4 = 1.6 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s. Flow has decreased by 60% because the gradient is smaller. The plant is taking up less water.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate flow rate with stomatal closure.
step_by_step.calculation: Stomatal closure raises leaf water potential to -0.6 MPa. ΔΨ = (-0.2) - (-0.6) = 0.4 MPa. J = 4 × 10⁻⁸ × 0.4 = 1.6 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s. The plant is conserving water by reducing transpiration, but photosynthesis is also reduced because stomata are closed.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Initial flow = 4 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s. (b) Drought reduces flow to 1.6 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s (60% reduction). (c) Stomatal closure reduces flow to 1.6 × 10⁻⁸ kg/s. This shows that both drought and stomatal closure reduce water flow. The plant can control flow by opening and closing stomata, balancing water loss against carbon gain.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Water flow depends on the water potential gradient between soil and leaf. When soil dries (Ψₛₒᵢₗ becomes more negative), the gradient decreases, and flow slows. The plant can also reduce flow by closing stomata, which raises Ψₗₑₐf. Understanding this helps predict how plants respond to drought.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The Journey of a Water Molecule
sections.introduction: A water molecule travels from the soil, through the plant, and into the atmosphere. Understanding this journey reveals how the xylem works.

process_flow.description: Water enters the root through root hairs and moves to the xylem.
process_flow.mechanism: Soil water (Ψ ≈ -0.1 MPa) → Root hair (osmosis) → Root cortex (through cells or between cells) → Endodermis (Casparian strip blocks between cells, forcing water through cells) → Xylem. Minerals are actively pumped into the xylem, lowering water potential and drawing water in. Root pressure (up to 0.5 MPa) can push water upward, especially at night.
process_flow.result: Water enters xylem in roots; minerals loaded; ready for upward transport.

image.alt: Water moving from soil through root hairs, cortex, endodermis, and into xylem
image.caption: Root uptake: Soil water → root hair → cortex → endodermis (Casparian strip) → xylem. Minerals are actively pumped in, drawing water by osmosis.

process_flow.description: Water moves upward through xylem vessels and tracheids, pulled by transpiration.
process_flow.mechanism: Xylem sap (Ψ ≈ -0.5 to -2.0 MPa) flows upward through stems and petioles to leaves. Flow is driven by tension from transpiration. Cohesion keeps the water column intact; adhesion prevents slippage. Flow rate depends on vessel diameter and number. Lateral flow through pits allows water to bypass blocked vessels.
process_flow.result: Water reaches leaves; minerals delivered; tension maintained.

image.alt: Water moving up through xylem in a stem, with arrows showing flow direction
image.caption: Xylem transport: Water moves upward through vessels and tracheids. Cohesion and adhesion keep the column intact. Flow is driven by tension from transpiration.

process_flow.description: Water evaporates from leaf cells and exits through stomata, creating tension.
process_flow.mechanism: Water leaves xylem veins → enters mesophyll cell walls → evaporates into air spaces (substomatal cavity) → exits through stomata → into atmosphere. Evaporation cools the leaf and creates tension that pulls water from the xylem. Stomata open (for CO₂) or close (to conserve water) based on light, CO₂, and water availability.
process_flow.result: Water lost to atmosphere; tension generated; CO₂ enters for photosynthesis.

image.alt: Water evaporating from leaf mesophyll and exiting through stomata
image.caption: Transpiration: Water evaporates from mesophyll cells into air spaces → exits through stomata → atmosphere. Evaporation creates tension that pulls water from xylem.

sections.definition: Cavitation (air bubbles in xylem) can block water flow. Plants have ways to prevent, contain, and repair cavitation.

process_types.energy_profile: Cavitation occurs when tension exceeds the water's ability to stay liquid. Air bubbles form, blocking vessels. This reduces hydraulic conductivity.
process_types.direction: Functional xylem (water-filled) → Tension increases → Air bubble forms → Vessel embolized (air-filled) → Reduced water flow
process_types.function: Cavitation is a risk of the cohesion-tension mechanism. Plants have evolved ways to prevent it (narrow vessels, torus-margo pits) and to contain it when it happens.
goals: Prevent cavitation (narrow vessels, strong pit membranes)
Contain cavitation (torus-margo pits seal off affected cells)
process_types.energy_source: Repair requires energy (ATP) from parenchyma cells to pump ions into embolized vessels, drawing water in.

primary_example.explanation:
Cavitation usually happens through pits. When tension is high, an air bubble can be "seeded" through a pit membrane from an air-filled cell into a water-filled cell. This is called air-seeding.

The pit membrane acts like a filter. If the pores in the membrane are large, air can be pulled through at lower tensions. If the pores are small, higher tension is needed. This is why narrow-pored pit membranes (like in conifers) are safer—they resist air-seeding.

In conifers, the torus-margo pit is especially safe. The torus seals the pit when pressure changes, preventing air from passing through. In angiosperms, pit membranes are more porous, making them more vulnerable to cavitation. This is one reason conifers can survive drought better than many angiosperms.

image.alt: A diagram showing air being pulled through a pit membrane into a water-filled vessel
image.caption: Air-seeding: Under high tension, air can be pulled through a pit membrane from an air-filled cell into a water-filled vessel, causing cavitation. Narrow pores resist air-seeding.

image.alt: A graph showing xylem conductivity decreasing as water potential drops (drought worsens)
image.caption: Cavitation impact: As drought worsens (more negative water potential), more xylem vessels cavitate, reducing water transport. Plants vary in how vulnerable they are to this loss.

process_types.energy_profile: Refilling embolized vessels requires energy to move water into air-filled tubes.
process_types.direction: Embolized vessel (air-filled) → Parenchyma cells pump ions → Water flows in → Air dissolves → Vessel functional again
process_types.function: Some plants can repair cavitated vessels, restoring hydraulic conductivity after drought. This allows them to survive repeated droughts.
goals: Generate osmotic gradient (parenchyma pumps ions into vessel)
process_types.energy_source: ATP from parenchyma respiration drives ion pumps (H⁺-ATPase) to lower water potential in embolized vessels.

primary_example.explanation:
Grapevines are known for their ability to repair embolized xylem. After a dry period, when water becomes available, the vine can refill its vessels. This happens at night, when transpiration is low and root pressure is high.

Parenchyma cells surrounding the embolized vessel pump ions into the vessel. This lowers the water potential in the vessel, drawing water from surrounding tissues. The water pressure builds up, compressing the air bubble until it dissolves. The vessel is then full of water and can conduct again.

Not all plants can do this. Some (like oaks) rely on growing new xylem each year rather than repairing old xylem. This is why oaks can be more vulnerable to repeated droughts—if they lose too many vessels, they cannot replace them until next spring.

image.alt: Parenchyma cells pumping ions into an embolized vessel, drawing water in
image.caption: Refilling: Parenchyma cells pump ions into embolized vessel → water follows → air bubble dissolves. Requires energy (ATP) and occurs at night when transpiration is low.

image.alt: A diagram comparing plants that can repair xylem (grapevine) and those that cannot (oak)
image.caption: Repair capacity: Some plants (grapevines) can refill embolized vessels. Others (oaks) must grow new xylem. This affects their ability to survive repeated droughts.

sections.relationship: Cavitation and repair represent vulnerability and resilience. Plants that can resist cavitation (narrow vessels, torus-margo pits) or repair it (refilling) are better able to survive drought. Understanding these processes helps predict how forests will respond to climate change.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding xylem. Knowing these terms will help you describe how plants transport water.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in xylem biology. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss plant water transport, drought responses, and xylem structure with confidence.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of xylem tissue with key terms: tracheid, vessel element, pit, perforation plate, lignin
image.caption: Key xylem terms: Knowing these structures helps you understand how water moves and how plants survive drought.

key_properties.explanation: The plant tissue that transports water and minerals from roots to leaves. Made of dead, hollow cells with lignified walls.
Long, tapered xylem cell with pits in its walls. Water moves between tracheids through pits. Found in all vascular plants; the only conducting cell in conifers and ferns.
Short, wide xylem cell with perforated end walls. Stacks to form continuous vessels. Found in flowering plants (angiosperms). Allows fast water flow.
A continuous tube made of vessel elements stacked end to end. Can be many meters long. Allows fast, efficient water flow.
A hard, waterproof polymer deposited in xylem cell walls. Provides strength, prevents collapse under tension, and makes wood hard.
A thin area in the xylem wall where only the primary wall remains. Allows water to move between adjacent xylem cells.
The end wall of a vessel element. Has one or more openings (perforations) that allow water to flow directly from one element to the next.

The explanation for how water moves up xylem. Transpiration creates tension (negative pressure) that pulls water up. Cohesion (water sticking to water) and adhesion (water sticking to xylem walls) keep the column intact.
The evaporation of water from leaves. Creates tension that pulls water up through xylem. Also cools the leaf.
A measure of the free energy of water. Measured in MPa. Water moves from high Ψ (less negative) to low Ψ (more negative). Soil Ψ > root Ψ > leaf Ψ > air Ψ drives water flow.
The formation of an air bubble in xylem under high tension. Blocks water flow. Can be caused by drought or freezing.
An air-filled xylem vessel that is no longer conducting water. Result of cavitation.
Wood with wide vessels in earlywood (spring) and narrow vessels or fibers in latewood (summer). Found in oaks, elms, ashes. Fast growth in spring, vulnerable to drought.
Wood with vessels of uniform size throughout the growth ring. Found in maples, birches, magnolias. Steady growth, less vulnerable to drought.
A specialized pit in conifer tracheids. The torus (disk) seals the pit when pressure changes, preventing air bubbles from spreading. Makes conifers safe but slow.
Pressure generated in roots by active ion pumping into xylem. Can push water upward, especially at night. Causes guttation (water droplets on leaf edges).
Water droplets that form on leaf edges when root pressure pushes water out of hydathodes (special pores). Occurs at night when transpiration is low.

summary:
Xylem is the plant tissue that transports water and minerals from roots to leaves. It is made of dead, hollow cells called tracheids and vessel elements. Tracheids are long and tapered, with pits that allow water to move between cells. Vessel elements are short and wide, with perforated end walls that form continuous tubes (vessels). Vessels allow fast water flow but are more vulnerable to air bubbles (cavitation) than tracheids.

Water moves through xylem by cohesion-tension. Transpiration (evaporation from leaves) creates tension (negative pressure) that pulls water up. Water molecules stick to each other (cohesion) and to xylem walls (adhesion), keeping the water column intact. This passive system uses no energy—the energy comes from the sun.

Xylem also provides support. The cell walls are thickened with lignin, a hard, waterproof polymer. Lignin prevents xylem from collapsing under tension and makes wood strong.

There is a trade-off between efficiency and safety in xylem. Wide vessels transport water fast but are vulnerable to cavitation. Narrow vessels are safe but slow. Plants balance these based on their environment. Ring-porous trees (oaks) have wide vessels in spring (fast growth) and narrow vessels in summer (drought safety). Diffuse-porous trees (maples) have uniform vessels. Conifers have only tracheids (safe but slow).

When drought causes cavitation, some plants can repair embolized vessels. Parenchyma cells pump ions into the blocked vessel, drawing water in and dissolving the air bubble. This requires energy and happens at night when transpiration is low.

Understanding xylem helps us understand how plants survive drought, how trees grow so tall, and how forests will respond to climate change. It is a remarkable system—a network of dead tubes, powered by the sun, that can lift water 100 meters or more.
